"Washington, D.C."), U.S. [38°53′13″N 77°1′34″W﻿ / ﻿38.88694°N 77.02611°W﻿ / 38.88694; -77.02611](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=United_States_Department_of_Energy&params=38_53_13_N_77_1_34_W_type:landmark_region:US-DC)

* [Chris Wright](/wiki/Chris_Wright "Chris Wright"), [Secretary](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Energy "United States Secretary of Energy") * [James Danly](/wiki/James_Danly "James Danly"), [Deputy Secretary](/wiki/United_States_Deputy_Secretary_of_Energy "United States Deputy Secretary of Energy")
* [Department of Energy Organization Act](/wiki/Department_of_Energy_Organization_Act "Department of Energy Organization Act")

(**DOE**) is an [executive department](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") of the [U.S. federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") that oversees national energy policy and [energy production](/wiki/Energy_production "Energy production"), the research and development of [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power"), the military's [nuclear weapons program](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_the_United_States "Nuclear weapons of the United States"), [nuclear reactor](/wiki/Nuclear_reactor "Nuclear reactor") production for the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"), energy-related research, and [energy conservation](/wiki/Energy_conservation "Energy conservation").

The **United States Department of Energy** (**DOE**) is an [executive department](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") of the [U.S. federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") that oversees national energy policy and [energy production](/wiki/Energy_production "Energy production"), the research and development of [nuclear power](/wiki/Nuclear_power "Nuclear power"), the military's [nuclear weapons program](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons_of_the_United_States "Nuclear weapons of the United States"), [nuclear reactor](/wiki/Nuclear_reactor "Nuclear reactor") production for the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy"), energy-related research, and [energy conservation](/wiki/Energy_conservation "Energy conservation").

The DOE was created in 1977 in the aftermath of the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis"). It sponsors more physical science research than any other U.S. federal agency, the majority of which is conducted through its system of [National Laboratories](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy_National_Laboratories "United States Department of Energy National Laboratories").[[3]](#cite_note-EnergySC-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The DOE also directs research in [genomics](/wiki/Genomics "Genomics"), with the [Human Genome Project](/wiki/Human_Genome_Project "Human Genome Project") originating from a DOE initiative.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The department is headed by the [secretary of energy](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Energy "United States Secretary of Energy"), who reports directly to the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and is a member of the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States "Cabinet of the United States"). The current secretary of energy is [Chris Wright](/wiki/Chris_Wright_(energy_executive) "Chris Wright (energy executive)"), who has served in the position since February 2025.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The department's headquarters are in southwestern [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), in the [James V. Forrestal Building](/wiki/James_V._Forrestal_Building "James V. Forrestal Building"), with additional offices in [Germantown, Maryland](/wiki/Germantown,_Maryland "Germantown, Maryland").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation and consolidation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation and consolidation")]

In 1942, during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the United States started the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project") to develop the [atomic bomb](/wiki/Atomic_bomb "Atomic bomb") under the [U.S. Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/U.S._Army_Corps_of_Engineers "U.S. Army Corps of Engineers"). After the war, in 1946, the [Atomic Energy Commission](/wiki/United_States_Atomic_Energy_Commission "United States Atomic Energy Commission") (AEC) was created to control the future of the project.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The [Atomic Energy Act of 1946](/wiki/Atomic_Energy_Act_of_1946 "Atomic Energy Act of 1946") also created the framework for the first [National Laboratories](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy_national_laboratories "United States Department of Energy national laboratories"). Among other nuclear projects, the AEC produced fabricated uranium fuel cores at locations such as [Fernald Feed Materials Production Center](/wiki/Fernald_Feed_Materials_Production_Center "Fernald Feed Materials Production Center") in [Cincinnati, Ohio](/wiki/Cincinnati,_Ohio "Cincinnati, Ohio").[[9]](#cite_note-NCLO950-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The [Energy Reorganization Act of 1974](/wiki/Energy_Reorganization_Act_of_1974 "Energy Reorganization Act of 1974") split the responsibilities of the AEC into the new [Nuclear Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Nuclear_Regulatory_Commission "Nuclear Regulatory Commission"), which was charged with regulating the nuclear power industry, and the [Energy Research and Development Administration](/wiki/Energy_Research_and_Development_Administration "Energy Research and Development Administration"), which was assigned to manage the nuclear weapon, naval reactor, and energy development programs.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") called attention to the need to consolidate energy policy.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 1977, President [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") signed into law the [Department of Energy Organization Act](/wiki/Department_of_Energy_Organization_Act "Department of Energy Organization Act"), which established the Department of Energy.[[16]](#cite_note-Relyea2003-16) The new agency, which began operations on October 1, 1977, consolidated the [Federal Energy Administration](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Administration "Federal Energy Administration"), the [Energy Research and Development Administration](/wiki/Energy_Research_and_Development_Administration "Energy Research and Development Administration"), the [Federal Power Commission](/wiki/Federal_Power_Commission "Federal Power Commission"), and programs of various other agencies. Former Secretary of Defense [James Schlesinger](/wiki/James_Schlesinger "James Schlesinger"), who served under Presidents Nixon and Ford during the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), was appointed as the first secretary.

President [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") proposed the Department of Energy with the goal of promoting energy conservation and energy independence, and developing alternative sources of energy to reduce the use of [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel").[[17]](#cite_note-17) With international energy's future uncertain for America, Carter acted quickly to have the department come into action the first year of his presidency. This was an extremely important issue of the time as the oil crisis was causing shortages and [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation").[[18]](#cite_note-18) With the [Three Mile Island accident](/wiki/Three_Mile_Island_accident "Three Mile Island accident"), Carter was able to intervene with the help of the department. Through the DOE, Carter was able to make changes within the [Nuclear Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Nuclear_Regulatory_Commission "Nuclear Regulatory Commission"), including improving management and procedures, since nuclear energy and weapons are responsibilities of the department.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Weapon plans stolen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Weapon plans stolen")]

Main article: [Wen Ho Lee](/wiki/Wen_Ho_Lee "Wen Ho Lee")

In December 1999, the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") began investigating how China acquired plans for a specific nuclear device. [Wen Ho Lee](/wiki/Wen_Ho_Lee "Wen Ho Lee"), a scientist at [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory"), was accused of passing nuclear secrets to the Chinese government. Before any formal charges, federal officials, including Energy Secretary [Bill Richardson](/wiki/Bill_Richardson "Bill Richardson"), publicly identified Lee as a suspect. The U.S. Congress held hearings on the DOE’s handling of the case, with some senators proposing that nuclear security be managed by an independent agency.[[20]](#cite_note-slate-20) Of the 59 charges brought against Lee, all but one were later dropped after investigators concluded the leaked plans could not have come from him. Lee later received a $1.6 million settlement from the federal government and several news organizations.[[21]](#cite_note-cnn-21)

In the wake of the scandal[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) and allegations that direct management by the Department of Energy had resulted in U.S. nuclear secrets being leaked to China, the [National Nuclear Security Administration](/wiki/National_Nuclear_Security_Administration "National Nuclear Security Administration") was proposed.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Originally proposed to be independent, NNSA was instead chartered as a semiautonomous agency within the Department of Energy to be headed by an administrator reporting to the secretary of energy.

### Loan guarantee program of 2005

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Loan guarantee program of 2005")]

In 2001, [American Solar Challenge](/wiki/American_Solar_Challenge "American Solar Challenge") was sponsored by the DOE and the [National Renewable Energy Laboratory](/wiki/National_Renewable_Energy_Laboratory "National Renewable Energy Laboratory").[[25]](#cite_note-25) After the 2005 race, the DOE discontinued its sponsorship.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Title XVII of [Energy Policy Act of 2005](/wiki/Energy_Policy_Act_of_2005 "Energy Policy Act of 2005") authorizes the DOE to issue loan guarantees to eligible projects that "avoid, reduce, or sequester air pollutants or anthropogenic emissions of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas")" and "employ new or significantly improved technologies as compared to technologies in service in the United States at the time the guarantee is issued".[[27]](#cite_note-27) In loan guarantees, a conditional commitment requires to meet an equity commitment, as well as other conditions, before the loan guarantee is completed.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In September 2008, the DOE, the [Nuclear Threat Initiative](/wiki/Nuclear_Threat_Initiative "Nuclear Threat Initiative") (NTI), the [Institute of Nuclear Materials Management](/wiki/Institute_of_Nuclear_Materials_Management "Institute of Nuclear Materials Management") (INMM), and the [International Atomic Energy Agency](/wiki/International_Atomic_Energy_Agency "International Atomic Energy Agency") (IAEA) partnered to develop and launch the [World Institute for Nuclear Security](/wiki/World_Institute_for_Nuclear_Security "World Institute for Nuclear Security") (WINS), an international [non-governmental organization](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") designed to provide a forum to share best practices in strengthening the security and safety of [nuclear](/wiki/Nuclear_material "Nuclear material") and [radioactive](/wiki/Radioactive "Radioactive") materials and facilities.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In December 2024, the Loan Programs Office announced it would extend the largest loan ever sanctioned – a $15 billion (US) low-interest loan to support the modernization of [Pacific Gas & Electric](/wiki/Pacific_Gas_and_Electric_Company "Pacific Gas and Electric Company")’s hydroelectric power structure, enhance transmission lines critical for renewable energy integration, data center operations, and the growing fleet of electric vehicles. Initially requested as a $30 billion (US) loan, the amount was reduced due to concerns over the company’s repayment capacity.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Genesis Mission and AI partnerships (2025)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Genesis Mission and AI partnerships (2025)")]

In November 2025, President Donald Trump signed an executive order launching the Genesis Mission, an initiative involving the Department of Energy and U.S. national laboratories to apply artificial intelligence and advanced computing to accelerate scientific research and support energy and national security-related capabilities.[[31]](#cite_note-31) On December 18, 2025, the Department of Energy announced collaboration agreements with 24 organizations to support the initiative.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The department is under the control and supervision of a [United States Secretary of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Energy "United States Secretary of Energy"), a political appointee of the [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"). The Energy Secretary is assisted in managing the department by a [United States Deputy Secretary of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Deputy_Secretary_of_Energy "United States Deputy Secretary of Energy"), also appointed by the president, who assumes the duties of the secretary in the secretary's absence. The department also has three under secretaries, each appointed by the president, who oversee the major areas of the department's work. The president also appoints seven officials with the rank of Assistant Secretary of Energy who have line management responsibility for major organizational elements of the department. The Energy Secretary assigns their functions and duties.

### Symbolism in the seal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Symbolism in the seal")]

Excerpt from the [Code of Federal Regulations](/wiki/Code_of_Federal_Regulations "Code of Federal Regulations"), in [Title 10: Energy](/wiki/Title_10_of_the_Code_of_Federal_Regulations "Title 10 of the Code of Federal Regulations"):[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The official seal of the Department of Energy "includes a green shield bisected by a gold-colored lightning bolt, on which is emblazoned a gold-colored symbolic [sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), [atom](/wiki/Atom "Atom"), [oil derrick](/wiki/Oil_derrick "Oil derrick"), [windmill](/wiki/Windmill "Windmill"), and [dynamo](/wiki/Dynamo "Dynamo"). It is crested by the white head of an [eagle](/wiki/Eagle "Eagle"), atop a white rope. Both appear on a blue field surrounded by [concentric](/wiki/Concentric "Concentric") circles in which the name of the agency, in gold, appears on a green background."

"The [eagle](/wiki/Eagle "Eagle") represents the care in planning and the purposefulness of efforts required to respond to the Nation's increasing demands for [energy](/wiki/Energy "Energy"). The [sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), [atom](/wiki/Atom "Atom"), [oil derrick](/wiki/Oil_derrick "Oil derrick"), [windmill](/wiki/Windmill "Windmill"), and [dynamo](/wiki/Dynamo "Dynamo") serve as representative technologies whose enhanced development can help meet these demands. The rope represents the cohesiveness in the development of the technologies and their link to our future capabilities. The [lightning](/wiki/Lightning "Lightning") bolt represents the power of the natural forces from which energy is derived and the Nation's challenge in harnessing the forces."

"The color scheme is derived from nature, symbolizing both the source of energy and the support of man's existence. The blue field represents [air](/wiki/Air "Air") and [water](/wiki/Water "Water"), green represents [mineral](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral") resources and the [earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") itself, and gold represents the creation of energy in the release of natural forces. By invoking this [symbolism](/wiki/Symbol "Symbol"), the color scheme represents the Nation's commitment to meet its energy needs in a manner consistent with the [preservation](/wiki/Conservation_(ethic) "Conservation (ethic)") of the [natural environment](/wiki/Natural_environment "Natural environment")."

### Nuclear weapons sites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Nuclear weapons sites")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Wfm_area51_map_en.png/250px-Wfm_area51_map_en.png)](/wiki/File:Wfm_area51_map_en.png)

A map that details the [federal land](/wiki/Federal_lands "Federal lands") in southern Nevada, showing what was formerly called the [Nevada Test Site](/wiki/Nevada_Test_Site "Nevada Test Site") - now the [Nevada National Security Site](/wiki/Nevada_National_Security_Site "Nevada National Security Site")

The [NNSA](/wiki/National_Nuclear_Security_Administration "National Nuclear Security Administration") has federal responsibility for the design, testing and production of all nuclear weapons. Under its supervision, contractors carry out its responsibilities at the following government-owned sites:[[45]](#cite_note-Nuclear_Matters_Handbook-45)

* Research, development, and manufacturing guidance: [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory")[[46]](#cite_note-46) and [Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Livermore_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory")[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* Engineering of the non-nuclear components and system integration: [Sandia National Laboratories](/wiki/Sandia_National_Laboratories "Sandia National Laboratories")[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* Manufacturing of key components: [Kansas City National Security Campus](/wiki/Kansas_City_National_Security_Campus "Kansas City National Security Campus"), [Savannah River Site](/wiki/Savannah_River_Site "Savannah River Site") and [Y-12 National Security Complex](/wiki/Y-12_National_Security_Complex "Y-12 National Security Complex").[[45]](#cite_note-Nuclear_Matters_Handbook-45)
* Testing: [Nevada National Security Site](/wiki/Nevada_National_Security_Site "Nevada National Security Site")[[45]](#cite_note-Nuclear_Matters_Handbook-45)
* Final weapon and warhead assembling and dismantling: [Pantex](/wiki/Pantex "Pantex")[[49]](#cite_note-49)

## Budget

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Budget")]

On May 7, 2009 President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") unveiled a $26.4 billion budget request for DOE for fiscal year (FY) 2010, including $2.3 billion for the DOE [Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy](/wiki/Office_of_Energy_Efficiency_and_Renewable_Energy "Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy") (EERE). That budget aimed to substantially expand the use of [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") sources while improving energy transmission infrastructure. It also proposed significant investments[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in [hybrids](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle") and [plug-in hybrids](/wiki/Plug-in_hybrid "Plug-in hybrid"), [smart grid](/wiki/Smart_grid "Smart grid") technologies, and scientific research and innovation.[[50]](#cite_note-energy1-50)

As part of the $789 billion economic stimulus package in the [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act") of 2009, Congress provided Energy with an additional $38.3 billion for fiscal years 2009 and 2010, adding about 75 percent to Energy's annual budgets. Most of the stimulus spending was in the form of grants and contracts.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] For fiscal year 2013, each of the operating units of the Department of Energy operated with the following budgets:[[51]](#cite_note-51)

| Division | Funding (billions) |
| --- | --- |
| Nuclear Security | $11.5 |
| Energy and Environment | $9.5 |
| Science | $4.9 |
| Management | $0.25 |
| Other | $0.85 |
| **Total** | **$28** |

In March 2018, Energy Secretary Rick Perry testified to a Senate panel about the Trump administration's DOE budget request for fiscal year 2019. The budget request prioritized nuclear security while making large cuts to [energy efficiency](/wiki/Efficient_energy_use "Efficient energy use") and renewable energy programs. The proposal was a $500 million increase in funds over fiscal year 2017. It "promotes innovations like a new Office of Cybersecurity, Energy Security, and Emergency Response (CESER) and gains for the Office of Fossil Energy. Investments would be made to strengthen the National Nuclear Security Administration and modernize the nuclear force, as well as in weapons activities and advanced computing." However, the budget for the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy would be lowered to $696 million under the plan, down from $1.3 billion in fiscal year 2017. Overall, the department's energy and related programs would be cut by $1.9 billion.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

## Programs and contracts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Programs and contracts")]

### Energy Savings Performance Contract

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Energy Savings Performance Contract")]

Main article: [Energy Savings Performance Contract](/wiki/Energy_Savings_Performance_Contract "Energy Savings Performance Contract")

Energy Savings Performance Contracts (ESPCs) are contracts under which a contractor designs, constructs, and obtains the necessary financing for an energy savings project, and the federal agency makes payments over time to the contractor from the savings in the agency's utility bills. The contractor guarantees the energy improvements will generate savings, and after the contract ends, all continuing cost savings accrue to the federal agency.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Energy Innovation Hubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Energy Innovation Hubs")]

Energy Innovation Hubs are [multi-disciplinary](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath"), meant to advance highly promising areas of energy science and technology from their early stages of research to the point that the risk level will be low enough for industry to commercialize[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:United_States_Department_of_Energy#Dubious "Talk:United States Department of Energy")*] the technologies.[[50]](#cite_note-energy1-50) The Consortium for Advanced Simulation of Light Water Reactors (CASL) was the first DOE Energy Innovation Hub established in July 2010, for the purpose of providing advanced modeling and simulation (M&S) solutions for commercial nuclear reactors.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The 2009 DOE budget includes $280 million to fund eight Energy Innovation Hubs, each of which is focused on a particular energy challenge. Two of the eight hubs are included in the [EERE](/wiki/Office_of_Energy_Efficiency_and_Renewable_Energy "Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy") budget and will focus on integrating smart materials, designs, and systems into buildings to better conserve energy and on designing and discovering new concepts and materials needed to convert solar energy into electricity. Another two hubs, included in the DOE Office of Science budget, were created to tackle the challenges of devising advanced methods of energy storage and creating fuels directly from sunlight without the use of plants or microbes. Yet another hub was made to develop "smart" materials to allow the electrical grid to adapt and respond to changing conditions.[[50]](#cite_note-energy1-50)

In 2012, the DOE awarded $120 million to the [Ames Laboratory](/wiki/Ames_Laboratory "Ames Laboratory") to start a new EIH, the Critical Materials Institute, which will focus on improving the supply of [rare earth elements](/wiki/Rare_earth_element "Rare earth element").[[55]](#cite_note-NetworkWorld_2013-55)

### Advanced Research Projects Agency-Energy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Advanced Research Projects Agency-Energy")]

See also: [ARPA-E](/wiki/ARPA-E "ARPA-E")

ARPA-E was officially created by the [America COMPETES Act](/wiki/America_COMPETES_Act "America COMPETES Act"), authored by Congressman [Bart Gordon](/wiki/Bart_Gordon "Bart Gordon"),[[56]](#cite_note-56) within the United States Department of Energy (DOE) in 2007, though without a budget. The initial budget of about $400 million was a part of the [economic stimulus bill](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009") of February 2009.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### High Performance Computing for Energy Innovation (HPC4EI)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: High Performance Computing for Energy Innovation (HPC4EI)")]

**[HPC4EI](/wiki/HPC4EI "HPC4EI")** is an initiative that leverages [supercomputing](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer") resources from DOE national laboratories to support industry partnerships in advancing energy-efficient manufacturing, materials development, and decarbonisation technologies.[[58]](#cite_note-HPC4EI-Site-58)[[59]](#cite_note-DOE-FY22-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_Energy&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Other")]

* DOE Isotope Program - coordinates isotope production[[61]](#cite_note-61)
* [Federal Energy Management Program](/wiki/Federal_Energy_Management_Program "Federal Energy Management Program")
* Foundation for Energy Security and Innovation - a [501(c)(3) organization](/wiki/501(c)(3)_organization "501(c)(3) organization") dedicated to supporting DOE research
* Fusion Energy Sciences - a program to research [nuclear fusion](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion"), with a yearly budget in 2020 of $670 million, with $250 million of that going to [ITER](/wiki/ITER "ITER")[[62]](#cite_note-SciMag_1-62)
* [GovEnergy](/wiki/GovEnergy "GovEnergy") - an annual event partly sponsored by the DOE
* Grid Deployment Office - a division dedicated to spreading adoption of grid-enhancing technologies and improving transmission permitting
* [National Science Bowl](/wiki/National_Science_Bowl "National Science Bowl") - a high school and middle school science knowledge competition
* [Solar Decathlon](/wiki/Solar_Decathlon "Solar Decathlon") - an international collegiate competition to design and build solar-powered houses
* [State Energy Program](/wiki/State_Energy_Program "State Energy Program")
* [Weatherization Assistance Program](/wiki/Weatherization_Assistance_Program "Weatherization Assistance Program")
